title: Eviction Timeline & Attorney Fees 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate eviction timeline and attorney fees by case type. Free tool for forecasting legal costs and lawsuit duration.
intro: An eviction lawsuit (unlawful detainer) is a court-ordered process to remove a tenant from a rental property. The timeline ranges from **30 days to 18+ months** depending on state law, case type, and court backlog. Attorney fees for landlords typically run **$500–$5,000** for uncontested cases and **$3,000–$15,000+** for contested ones. This calculator estimates both variables based on your case type (non-payment, lease violation, holdover, or no-fault), helping landlords and tenants budget legal costs and plan accordingly before filing or responding to an eviction action.
keyTakeaway: **Total Eviction Cost ≈ Filing Fee ($50–$400) + Attorney Fees ($500–$15,000) + Lost Rent ($1,200–$3,000/month × months pending)**. For a contested non-payment case in a high-cost state, total exposure often exceeds $20,000.
useCases: A landlord whose tenant stopped paying rent 3 months ago needs to know how long the eviction will take before they can re-rent the unit.
A tenant who received a 3-day notice wants to understand the full court timeline so they can negotiate a move-out date or find an attorney.
A property management company budgeting annual legal costs wants to estimate attorney fee exposure across a portfolio of 50+ units.
An investor evaluating a rental property in a rent-controlled city needs to understand how long a no-fault eviction (owner move-in) takes before they can occupy the unit.
A paralegal preparing an unlawful detainer complaint needs reference data on statutory notice periods and filing deadlines by case type.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The eviction timeline is built from **five sequential phases**, each with a legally mandated minimum duration. Attorney fees are estimated using market rate data from state bar surveys and legal aid benchmarks.

```
Total Timeline (days) =
  Notice Period
  + Court Filing & Summons Service (5–15 days)
  + Tenant Response Window (5–10 days)
  + Hearing Wait Time (14–90 days, varies by county)
  + Writ of Possession & Sheriff Lockout (3–21 days)
  [+ Appeal / Continuance Period if contested: +30–180 days]

Attorney Fee Estimate =
  Base Retainer ($500–$2,500)
  + Hourly Rate ($150–$450/hr) × Estimated Hours
  + Court Filing Fee ($50–$400)
  + Optional: Process Server ($75–$200), Expert Witness ($500+)
```

Uncontested cases average **4–8 attorney hours**; contested cases average **15–40+ hours**.

---

## Reference Table

| Case Type | Statutory Notice | Avg. Total Timeline | Avg. Attorney Fees (Uncontested) | Avg. Attorney Fees (Contested) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Non-Payment of Rent | 3–14 days | 30–90 days | $800–$2,500 | $3,000–$10,000 |
| Lease Violation (Curable) | 3–30 days to cure | 45–120 days | $1,000–$3,000 | $4,000–$12,000 |
| Lease Violation (Incurable) | 3–30 days to vacate | 30–90 days | $800–$2,500 | $3,500–$10,000 |
| Holdover Tenancy | 30–60 days | 60–150 days | $1,200–$3,500 | $5,000–$15,000 |
| No-Fault / Owner Move-In | 60–120 days | 90–180 days | $1,500–$4,000 | $6,000–$18,000 |
| Illegal Subletting | 3–10 days | 30–75 days | $1,000–$2,500 | $3,000–$9,000 |

> **Note:** California, New York, and New Jersey have significantly longer timelines (6–18 months for contested cases) due to tenant protection laws and court backlog.

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Non-Payment, Texas (Uncontested)
A landlord in Houston issues a **3-day Pay or Quit notice**. The tenant does not pay. The landlord files in Justice of the Peace court (~$121 filing fee). A hearing is set within **10 days**. Default judgment is entered. Sheriff lockout occurs within **7 days**. **Total timeline: ~21–30 days. Attorney fees: ~$800–$1,200** if using a local eviction attorney on a flat-fee basis.

### Case 2 — Non-Payment, California (Contested)
Landlord issues a **3-day Pay, Perform, or Quit notice**. Tenant responds with an answer citing habitability defenses (warranty of habitability, Cal. Civ. Code §1942). Case goes to trial. Multiple continuances are requested. **Total timeline: 6–12 months. Attorney fees: $6,000–$14,000** for the landlord, potentially mirrored by tenant legal aid costs. Lost rent during proceedings: $2,500/mo × 8 months = **$20,000**.

### Case 3 — No-Fault / Owner Move-In, New York City
Under NYC Local Law, landlord must provide **90-day written notice** in most cases. Tenant contests the eviction claiming retaliatory motive. Housing Court scheduling backlog adds 4–6 months. **Total timeline: 9–18 months. Attorney fees: $8,000–$20,000**. Landlord may also owe the tenant **1 month's rent as relocation assistance** in certain jurisdictions (e.g., LA, San Francisco).

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Skipping or miscounting the notice period.** Serving a 3-day notice on a Friday when weekends don't count in your state restarts the clock. Courts dismiss cases on this technicality regularly. Always verify whether your state counts calendar days or business days (e.g., California uses calendar days; some NY notices use business days).

2. **Filing the wrong case type.** Landlords sometimes file for non-payment when the actual issue is an unauthorized pet (a curable lease violation). This changes the required notice from 3 days to 10–30 days in many states and can result in dismissal.

3. **Underestimating the contested timeline.** Many landlords plan around the 30-day uncontested estimate. When a tenant files an answer or requests a jury trial, timelines triple or quadruple. Budget for the contested scenario as your financial worst case.

4. **Ignoring local rent control and "just cause" ordinances.** Over 100 U.S. cities have local eviction control laws stricter than state law (e.g., Los Angeles RSO, NYC Rent Stabilization, San Francisco Rent Ordinance). Filing without "just cause" in a covered unit results in immediate dismissal and potential attorney fee sanctions against the landlord under fee-shifting statutes.

5. **Not accounting for the CDC/state moratorium history.** While the federal CDC moratorium ended August 26, 2021 (per *Alabama Association of Realtors v. HHS*), several states (CA, NY, NJ) maintained their own moratoriums into 2022–2023. Check your state's current status before assuming you can file immediately.

6. **Neglecting to document properly.** Courts require proof of notice service (certificate of mailing, process server affidavit, or personal delivery declaration). Missing documentation is the #1 reason for eviction case continuances.

---

faq.q: How long does a typical eviction take from start to finish?
faq.a: In fast states like Texas or Georgia, an uncontested non-payment eviction can be completed in **21–45 days** from notice to lockout. In slow states like California, New York, or New Jersey, contested evictions routinely take **6–18 months** due to statutory notice requirements, court scheduling backlogs, and tenant right-to-cure periods. The national median for all eviction cases (contested and uncontested combined) is approximately **45–75 days** according to Princeton University's Eviction Lab data.

faq.q: What is the average attorney fee for an eviction lawsuit?
faq.a: Flat-fee eviction services (common for uncontested cases) typically range from **$500–$1,500** depending on state and complexity. Hourly-rate attorneys charge **$150–$450/hour**, and contested cases requiring hearings, discovery, or trials average **15–40 hours**, putting total fees at **$3,000–$15,000+**. In high-cost markets like NYC or San Francisco, landlord attorneys routinely bill **$350–$500/hour**, and complex cases exceed $20,000 in legal fees alone.

faq.q: Can a landlord recover attorney fees from the tenant?
faq.a: Only if the lease contains an **attorney fee clause** AND the landlord wins. Many states (e.g., California under Civil Code §1717) make such clauses **reciprocal by law**, meaning the tenant can also recover fees if they prevail. In practice, collecting a fee award from an evicted tenant is difficult — most lack assets. Some states (FL, TX) allow prevailing party fee recovery in eviction cases by statute even without a lease clause.

faq.q: What is the required notice period for a non-payment eviction?
faq.a: It varies by state: **3 days** (CA, FL, TX, NV), **5 days** (IL, OH, WI), **7 days** (GA, SC, NC), **10 days** (CO, WA), **14 days** (OR, ME), or **30 days** in some local jurisdictions. These are minimum statutory periods; lease agreements cannot shorten them but may lengthen them. Always serve notice in the legally prescribed manner (personal service, posting-and-mailing, certified mail) or it won't start the clock.

faq.q: What's the difference between an uncontested and contested eviction?
faq.a: An **uncontested eviction** occurs when the tenant does not file a written answer with the court — the landlord typically receives a default judgment and the process moves quickly (21–60 days). A **contested eviction** means the tenant files an answer raising defenses (habitability, retaliation, discrimination, procedural errors), triggering a full hearing or trial. Contested cases cost **3–5× more** in attorney fees and take **3–10× longer** to resolve.

faq.q: Are there free or low-cost legal resources for tenants facing eviction?
faq.a: Yes. The **Legal Services Corporation (LSC)** funds 132 legal aid organizations nationwide that provide free civil legal help to income-eligible tenants (generally ≤125% of federal poverty level). HUD-approved housing counseling agencies (search at hud.gov) offer free advice. Many cities also have **tenant right-to-counsel programs** — New York City, Philadelphia, Cleveland, and Newark guarantee free attorneys to income-eligible tenants in eviction court.

faq.q: Does filing for bankruptcy stop an eviction?
faq.a: Filing Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 bankruptcy triggers an **automatic stay** under 11 U.S.C. §362, which temporarily halts most eviction proceedings. However, if a landlord already has a **judgment for possession** before the bankruptcy filing, the stay may not apply (11 U.S.C. §362(b)(22)). Landlords can also file a **motion to lift the stay**, which courts often grant within 30 days in eviction cases. Bankruptcy delays eviction by **30–90 days** in most scenarios, not indefinitely.

faq.q: Can a landlord evict a tenant without going to court (self-help eviction)?
faq.a: No. **Self-help evictions are illegal in all 50 states.** Changing locks, removing doors, shutting off utilities, or removing the tenant's belongings without a court order exposes the landlord to **civil liability** — typically **2–3× actual damages plus attorney fees** (e.g., California Civil Code §789.3 allows recovery of $100/day up to $2,000 plus actual damages). Some states classify self-help eviction as a misdemeanor criminal offense. Always obtain a Writ of Possession and use the sheriff for lockout.

faq.q: How does the eviction process affect a tenant's credit score?
faq.a: The eviction filing itself does **not** directly appear on credit reports. However, if a money judgment is entered (for unpaid rent or damages), it can appear in public records searches used by tenant screening companies (e.g., TransUnion SmartMove, CoreLogic SafeRent) and remain accessible for **7 years**. Unpaid rent sent to collections DOES impact credit scores — a collection account can lower a FICO score by **50–150 points**. Additionally, eviction records in court databases are visible to future landlords through background checks.
